content:
5 min read
Just now

--

A few years ago, I finally reached a point where frontend development felt natural.
Not because I knew every framework or memorized every API, but because I understood why things behaved the way they did. I knew why browsers rendered the same CSS differently. I knew which animations would stay smooth at 60 FPS and which would quietly kill performance. I understood JavaScript quirks that most developers never notice because frameworks hide them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It took years to get there.
And just when I started feeling genuinely confident in my specialization, the conversation changed.
“Frontend developers should become full-stack.” “AI can generate most frontend code.” “Specialists are becoming less valuable.”
I wasn’t being told my knowledge was wrong. I was being told it wasn’t enough anymore. That’s a strange feeling. You spend years climbing one mountain, only to discover that everyone has already started hiking a different one.
The Frontend Developer Path:

• First it was Redux and state management. I learned that managing application state is often more difficult than building the UI itself.

• Then TypeScript and stronger typing. I realized that writing JavaScript was one thing, but designing maintainable code for large applications was another.

• Then PWAs, browser APIs, and IndexedDB. Suddenly, web applications were expected to work offline and behave more like native apps.

• Then Observables. Understanding the difference between Promises and Observables — and reactive programming in general — took me much longer than I expected.

• Then authentication. JWTs, refresh tokens, sessions, OAuth… I finally understood that frontend isn’t only about rendering pages; it’s also about trust and security.

• Then React Native, Capacitor, and Flutter. Every solution promised “write once, run everywhere,” but each came with its own trade-offs.

• Then monorepos. I learned that organizing a growing codebase can be just as challenging as writing the code itself.

• Then performance. Lazy loading, code splitting, bundle size, rendering performance, and learning why some applications feel instant while others don’t.

• Then communication beyond REST with GraphQL and gRPC. Different problems require different ways of exchanging data.

• Then algorithms and data structures. I wanted to understand what happened under the hood, and I even ended up using tree traversal algorithms while building one of my own components.

• Then browser APIs. I discovered just how much functionality modern browsers expose if you know where to look.

• Then Web Components. Learning how to build framework-agnostic components helped me better understand what React, Angular, and Vue abstract away.

• Then Sass. And a few years later… back to modern CSS because it had evolved enough to replace many of the reasons I used Sass in the first place.

• Then StencilJS. The idea of building a design system once and exporting it to multiple frontend frameworks completely changed how I thought about reusable UI.

• Then I spent four months building a calendar component. It was one of the first moments I genuinely felt like a real frontend engineer because I had to combine performance, accessibility, state management, and countless edge cases into a single component.

• Then I realized I still didn’t fully understand the JavaScript event loop. That was humbling enough that I went back and started studying JavaScript fundamentals from scratch again.

• Then frontend security. XSS, sanitization, CSRF, CSP… I learned that writing secure frontend code is just as important as writing functional code.

• Then Node.js, NestJS, and the Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern. My first serious step into backend development.

• Then Qwik and Vue.js. Working outside the Angular ecosystem taught me that every framework has different strengths, but they all solve many of the same underlying problems.
I would love to go deeper and continue my fe journey with:

• Learn more about canvas

• Try to code some dummy framework myself to understand more about the immediate changes of JavaScript

• Go deeper into hydration and SSR topics
But with family and other hobbies and some personal life + the full-stack transition (that is suddenly needed) — it is just impossible to make the day 30 hours long.
My Strategy was:
Before we all heard about the T-shaped developer, the theory was to master one thing, and then do a little bit of other “things” and add them up one by one by priority, but with a concentration on FE, for example, and add a bit of DevOps, Linux admin, BE, etc.
But now everything feels a bit different
Now the managers say that I should be a generalist.
But my brain just cannot handle it. I cannot just blindly guide some Agent to do my work. I might understand the basics of SQL, controllers, and some basic BE logic, but I haven’t dived deep into BE -> and my brain just doesn’t work in a way to do something randomly — I need to understand some topic at least to 80% to be able to create something — if I don’t understand it, then I know the output might be wrong or weird, or I won’t create an optimal solution. And that drives me crazy.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I understand that I need to learn more about BE architecture. To start: I might learn more about OOP -> I have some knowledge, but not deep enough, as on FE there is “composition over inheritance”.
I might learn more about database optimizations and indexing and try something.
I might learn about hexagonal architecture. But I just don’t know which language I should choose, why some systems are written in Python and not Node.js, what the difference is between Java + Spring vs. C# and .NET, and details like that.
And I know that if I want to understand these things to that level, I would need about the same experience as I have with FE -> maybe not 10 years, but for sure another 5 years with various tech on BE and various situations, which is just not possible.
So The Strategy:
I decided to still concentrate heavily on FE, but being “good enough” with the BE. Then I will take very small steps forward and learn new topics step by step. But not rush into it, as it is just not possible.
Conclusion
Being a Software Engineer is a hard path. But if you understand the pain I was describing, you can smile for a moment because it means that you fought hard. In today’s world of change, we need to look back more often, we need to take a rest, and learn the basics again and again. We shouldn’t rush into every new trend immediately but be very cautious.
Wishing you a great day, and do not forget why you started programming — it should be fun! :)
